analysis:
- Common pipeline: Pareto prefilter → cheap box-volume ± weight‑vector scalar proxy → k‑proportional shortlist → CELF/lazy‑greedy or greedy on shortlist → cached singleton (sometimes pair) HVs → bounded 1‑for‑1 swap local search → deterministic padding. The best heuristics (1–5) vary shortlist sizing and how aggressively they compute exact HVs.
- HV-call cost dominates runtime. Stronger variants reduce expensive calls via: CELF timestamping, singleton/pair caches, upper‑bound pruning (box volume), and hv‑call budgets. Heuristic 1 trades more exact greedy HVs on a very small shortlist then local swaps — simple and effective but can waste calls if shortlist grows.
- Data reuse: cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caches appear in better designs; vectorized scalar scoring reduces Python overhead. Weak spots: repeated vstack/array copies, linear "in idx in list" membership checks, and O(N^2) nondominated loops that could be vectorized or indexed.
- Determinism and tie‑breaking are handled consistently (fixed seeds, stable sorts), which helps reproducibility but some routines still shuffle unnecessarily.
- Avoidable overheads in weaker heuristics: recomputing approx volumes, recreating arrays inside loops, full HV recomputation for every candidate selection without lazy updates, and lack of set membership for O(1) checks.
- Algorithmic tradeoffs: larger shortlists and denser weight probes improve solution quality but increase HV calls; CELF-style lazy evaluation with tight upper bounds gives the best quality/performance sweet spot. Local swaps are useful but should be limited and use cached deltas when possible.
experience: Prefer a Pareto‑filtered, vectorized scoring to form an O(k) shortlist; run CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pair) HVs, prune via cheap box‑volume upper bounds, enforce an HV‑call budget, and finish with a tiny deterministic swap pass. Optimize data reuse (index slicing, sets for membership), minimize array copies, and adapt shortlist size to pareto/k to balance quality and HV cost.
current_reflection: Prefer a Pareto‑filtered, vectorized scoring to form an O(k) shortlist; run CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pair) HVs, prune via cheap box‑volume upper bounds, enforce an HV‑call budget, and finish with a tiny deterministic swap pass. Optimize data reuse (index slicing, sets for membership), minimize array copies, and adapt shortlist size to pareto/k to balance quality and HV cost.
good_reflections: Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.

Keep the four-stage pipeline, but (a) vectorize everything possible (precompute ref−pts, rect volumes), (b) use sets/boolean masks not list membership for O(1) checks, (c) CELF+singleton cache+selective pairwise cache minimises HV calls, (d) use an adaptive O(k) shortlist (cap by Pareto size), and (e) expose an HV-call budget and an approximate HVC fallback (many-weight scalarization) to trade quality vs time deterministically.

Prefer a compact, high‑precision shortlist (fast HVC proxy) + CELF with singleton (and tiny pairwise) HV caches. Precompute ref−points and box volumes, use vectorized Pareto filtering, lexicographic stable ordering, and sets for membership. Lazily recompute true marginal gains only when upper bounds indicate promise, cap HV calls, and prune swap checks by box/HVC upper bounds. These give reproducible, high‑quality k‑subsets with far fewer expensive hypervolume evaluations.

Design heuristics to minimize exact HV evaluations. Use strong, vectorized proxies to form an adaptive O(k) shortlist, run CELF with singleton caches and timestamps, selectively cache pairwise HVs only for tiny shortlists, prune by cheap upper bounds, maintain preallocated selected buffers and boolean masks/sets, and enforce a strict HV‑call budget plus small deterministic swap budget. This yields near‑optimal HV per cost while keeping runtime predictable.

Favor a CELF lazy greedy on a well‑constructed adaptive shortlist (box_vol × scalarization) with cached singleton and selective pairwise HVs, tight box-based upper‑bound pruning, and an hv-call budget scaled by k and shortlist size. Use vectorized dominance checks, sets for membership, incremental HV updates (avoid repeated vstack), and a small bounded deterministic swap pass. Parallelize expensive HV evaluations where possible.
bad_reflections: Favor a hybrid score (box volume × multi‑weight scalarization), build an adaptive shortlist (k‑ and pareto‑aware), cache singleton HVs plus small pairwise tables, use CELF lazy greedy with box upper‑bound pruning, cap HV calls, then run a small bounded deterministic swap (incremental HV updates). Vectorize dominance/scoring, preallocate buffers, and avoid expensive Python list operations for membership to get better quality/runtime.

Design candidates by (1) Pareto prefilter + deterministic blended box+scalar shortlist sized O(k); (2) CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and small selective pairwise cache; (3) tight box/HV upper‑bound pruning and an HV‑call budget with box‑fallback estimates; (4) incremental or cached local‑swap evaluations; and (5) vectorized filters and set membership to cut Python overhead. Tune shortlist size and weight probes for the budget/instance.

Design shortlists adaptively (k-proportional, diversity-aware), use CELF with cached singletons and timestamps, prune with cheap box-volume upper bounds, limit pairwise caches to tiny shortlists, and enforce an explicit HV-call budget. Reuse precomputed ref-diffs and avoid Python-level O(n) membership/reshaping in inner loops; prefer boolean masks and batched NV operations. These changes yield most HV-quality gains per unit runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto filter, O(k) adaptive shortlist, vectorized proxies (box_vol × multi‑weight scalarization), CELF lazy greedy, singleton + selective pairwise HV cache, box upper bounds, HV‑call budget, deterministic swap.  
- Advice: Build a Pareto‑filtered, vectorized O(k) shortlist; run CELF with cached singleton (and tiny pairwise) HVs, lazily recomputing gains only when box/upper bounds permit; enforce an explicit HV‑call budget with a many‑weight fallback; finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap. Precompute ref‑diffs, use boolean masks/sets, and minimize array copies.  
- Avoid: repeated exact HV calls, Python O(n) membership checks, unbounded pairwise caches, excessive array copying, and nondeterministic final tuning.  
- Explanation: Cuts expensive hypervolume evaluations, yields reproducible, predictable runtime, and delivers near‑optimal k‑subsets per compute cost.
